data.title: AI, Energy And Everything In Between: Thematics In July 2025
data.description:
Building on the launch of the inaugural S&P Thematics Dashboard, the July edition analyzes the monthly performance of a broad set of themes to uncover key market dynamics. Designed as a thematic market lens, the dashboard—developed in collaboration with Theia Insights—offers rich perspectives on market performance by leveraging nearly 200 themes shaping the global economy. It highlights top- and bottom-performing themes, integrates macro and sentiment indicators and delivers multi-lens insights across regions, sectors, megatrends and factors.

This month’s thematic performance was shaped by AI-driven infrastructure demand, renewed investor enthusiasm for precision health technologies and sustained momentum in the energy transition space. In contrast, traditional healthcare segments (e.g., Healthcare Insurance) and consumer-linked themes underperformed. Additionally, recent legislation related to the crypto sector fueled investor optimism across much of the crypto ecosystem—particularly within the Stablecoins theme.

Infrastructure of Intelligence: AI Push Fuels Computing Demand

At the top of the leaderboard, Data Centers & High-Performance Computing posted a standout 13.40% monthly return, adding to its YTD surge (up 32.06%). Meta (META) and Google’s (GOOGL) multi-billion-dollar commitments to AI infrastructure, referred to as “AI factories,” were received positively by the markets. Their revenues showed gains partly due to this significant capital expenditure. Limited access to reliable power and grid capacity for energy-intensive AI infrastructure, once a critical bottleneck, is now being actively addressed through a wave of investment in nuclear energy and renewable-plus-storage solutions. These themes also delivered strong returns in July, as outlined later in the document.

Adjacent AI infrastructure sectors also benefited, with Optical Communications & Optoelectronics rising 10.65% to deliver strong double-digit returns, driven by record-setting breakthroughs in data transmission speeds and silicon photonics integration.

Healthcare Divide: Innovation Outperforms while Services Falter

A sharp divergence defined the healthcare space.

On one end, innovation-led biotech themes gained meaningful traction.

  • Cannabis & Psychedelics rose 13.23%, helped by clinical data validating psilocybin’s therapeutic potential and regulatory progress in Europe.
  • Genomics & Gene Editing gained 11.59%, as breakthroughs in AI-enabled gene diagnostics and newborn genome screening expanded the sector’s relevance.
  • Cancer Therapy also performed strongly, rising 10.36%, buoyed by developments in precision medicine.

In contrast, Health Insurance recorded the steepest decline, at –10.71%. Growing concern over rising costs for insurers and regulatory scrutiny, weighed heavily on traditional healthcare companies. The Pharmacy theme also decreased 9.92%, as falling reimbursement rates, inflation, softer consumer spending and rising competition continue to squeeze margins.

Energy Storage and “Old” Fuel Find New Purpose

Batteries & Energy Storage gained 11.61%, continuing a four-month streak of positive returns. The surge was fueled by accelerating grid-scale storage deployments, particularly those utilizing lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries, which supported lithium demand while reducing reliance on more expensive nickel and cobalt chemistries. This dynamic favored companies operating across the value chain, from lithium producers to residential and utility-scale integrators.

Coal’s 9.12% rise marked an interesting shift, underpinned by supportive policy dynamics and news of potential curbs on Chinese coal production. Nuclear Energy (up 6.72%) and Wind Energy (up 7.47%) also saw solid performance, suggesting broad investor interest across the traditional-renewable spectrum to achieve energy transition goals.

Solar Energy, however, lagged at 5.10%, pointing to investor caution amid policy uncertainty and a slowdown in new utility-scale installations following the rollback of clean energy subsidies.

Stablecoins Go Mainstream: The Corporate Adoption Wave

Fueled by landmark regulation, notably the U.S. GENIUS Act, the stablecoin market—expected to grow beyond USD 190 billion—is shifting from speculative assets to an essential backbone of the crypto economy. Global corporations are evaluating stablecoin payment integrations to streamline costs and enhance international settlements, particularly in emerging economies.

In parallel, Europe’s MiCA regulation is unlocking stablecoin activity across the continent, offering legal clarity for euro-denominated tokens and driving participation from traditional financial institutions. Major banks are piloting stablecoin issuance, custody and trading services to modernize payments and reduce reliance on legacy rails.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ADMA Biologics: A Better Quarter Than Headline Numbers
data.description:
Today, I am putting ADMA Biologics, Inc. (NASDAQ:ADMA) in the spotlight for the first time in 2025. This unique healthcare concern saw its stock stage a significant pullback after posting sector quarter results after the bell on Wednesday of this week. The stock ended down nearly 10% at market close Thursday, after being down 20% early in the trading day. Buying opportunity or is the stock not in the 'buy zone' yet? An analysis follows below.

ADMA Biologics, Inc. is headquartered in New Jersey. The company operates a network of 10 plasma collection facilities in the U.S., the last of which was completed in 2023. It manufactures two key products from this collection, BIVIGAM and ASCENIV. With the post quarterly earnings plunge in the stock, the shares trade for around $16.50 a share and sport an approximate market capitalization just north of $4 billion.

Second Quarter Results:

ADMA Biologics, Inc. posted its Q2 numbers on August 6th. The company delivered GAAP earnings of 14 cents a share, in line with the consensus. GAAP net income for the quarter came in at $34.2 million, a seven percent increase over 2Q2024. Importantly, adjusted EBITDA rose 59% to $50.8 million not including a non-recurring item (a $12.6 million Medicaid rebate accrual reversal) that occurred in the same period a year ago. Gross margins improved to 55.1% from 53.6% but would have rose 7.7% if not for that non-recurring item from the prior period a year ago. Adjusted net income was $36 million, up 89% from 2Q2024 sans that non-recuring item.

Revenues rose nearly 14% on a year-over-year basis to $122 million, just a tad over expectations. Revenues would have been up 29% over the same quarter a year ago except for that non-recurring item in the previous period. The company did not provide a sales breakdown, but the majority of revenues came from ASCENIV. Importantly, during the quarter, the company initiated an FDA approved manufacturing change that will provide a 20% yield enhancement.

Management reaffirmed FY2025 and FY2026 guidance around all key metrics including revenue, net income and adjusted EBITDA. Leadership also projected it can achieve $1.1 billion in annual revenues by FY2030. In addition, management also clarified that 'Potential upside from the FDA-approved yield enhancement process remains excluded from 2025 guidance and is conservatively reflected in 2026 projections'. The same applied to the FY2030 $1.1 billion revenue projection. Nearer term, management expects at least $625 million in total revenue, adjusted EBITDA of $340 million or more and adjusted net income of at least $245 million in FY2026.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Approximately six percent of the outstanding float in the shares are currently held short. Insiders and beneficial owners of the stock have sold just over $10 million collectively worth of equity so far in 2025. Analyst firms have not yet chimed in since second quarter results hit the wires. Following first quarter results, Cantor Fitzgerald ($25 price target), Mizuho Securities ($35 price target, up previously from $32) and Raymond James ($32 price target, up from $25 previously) all reissued Buy ratings.

After the quarter closed, ADMA Biologics executed a syndicated debt refinancing led by J.P. Morgan (effective August 5th). Management stated this refinancing 'should meaningfully reduce ADMA's borrowing costs while enhancing liquidity and strategic optionality through a new $225 million revolving credit facility'.

The company listed just over $90 million of cash and a tad over $73 million in senior notes on its balance in the 10-K, management filed for Q2. In addition, the company implemented a $500 million stock buyback authorization in the second quarter, that would take approximately 12% of the shares out of the float at current trading levels. ADMA Biologics bought back $15 million of its own stock in Q2.

Conclusion:

ADMA Biologics made 49 cents a share on just over $426 million in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus has earnings rising to 58 cents a share in FY2025 as sales climb to $506 million. They project sales to move up substantially to 93 cents a share in FY2026 on revenue growth of 25%.

The stock seems to have been unfairly punished on Thursday as ADMA Biologics only met top and bottom-line expectations. Margins are improving impressively and should increase additional on new improved manufacturing yield. Guidance for FY2025 and FY2026 is likely to be taken up once the full impacts of the more effective process are fully known.

The company has more favorable financing and just put in place a large stock buyback program. Revenue over the next couple of years will be in the low to mid 20s and ADMA Biologics will become increasing profitable at a solid clip. In summary, yesterday's market action seems a bit of an overreaction. ADMA remains a solid 'accumulate' and I added to my stake in the stock via covered call orders early Thursday when the shares briefly dipped under $15.00 a share.

data.title: fuboTV: This Show Isn't Over Just Yet
data.description:
August 8th was a really good day for shareholders of fuboTV Inc. (NYSE:FUBO). Shares of the company rose around 5% after management announced, before the market opened, financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Management reported revenue, earnings per share, and adjusted earnings per share, that all exceeded analysts’ expectations. On top of this, subscriber numbers came in better than expected, and management is looking forward to completing its merger with Hulu + Live TV in a deal that will see The Walt Disney Company (DIS) come to own 70% of the combined business.

Back in May of this year, I called the company a Buy candidate. Since then, the stock has skyrocketed by 65.7%, easily outperforming the 12.9% rise that the S&P 500 saw. This move higher comes amidst improving financial results, at least in some respects, and the realization that the combination of the company with Disney’s Hulu + Live TV will prove to be a game changer that will create value for shareholders in the long run. Even though I think the easy money has been made by now, I do think that the company deserves a bit more upside. Because of this, I think that having it rated a soft ‘buy’ is logical at this time.

A solid quarter

Fundamentally speaking, fuboTV is doing better than what analysts have been anticipating. For the second quarter of 2025, revenue came in at $380 million. That does represent a 2.8% drop from the $391 million the company reported one year earlier. However, it happens to be $12.9 million above what analysts were expecting.

This drop in sales came as the number of subscribers in North America declined from 1.45 million last year to 1.36 million this year. This took North American revenue down from $382.7 million to $371.3 million. Management has stopped reporting monthly ARPU (average revenue per user). But based on my own estimates, it should have risen by about 3.7% year over year. It is worth pointing out that management was only expecting subscribers of between 1.225 million and 1.255 million. So that does mark a significant win for the company. For its international operations, subscriber count dropped from 399,000 to 349,000. Even so, revenue increased 4.7% year over year. But at just $8.7 million for the second quarter of 2025, it is basically a rounding error. Just like with the operations in North America, the international side of the business did better than expected from a subscriber perspective. Management had been anticipating subscribers amounting to between 325,000 and 335,000.

On the bottom line, the company showed some improvements. The company went from losing $0.08 per share to losing $0.02 per share, taking its net loss from $25.3 million to $8 million. The earnings per share reported by the company ended up being $0.01 better than what analysts anticipated. Adjusted earnings per share, meanwhile, went from negative $0.04 to positive $0.05. It ultimately ended up being $0.02 above what analysts were hoping to see. This translated to arise from negative $13.4 million to positive $18.1 million when it comes to adjusted net profits.

Most other profitability metrics improved also. Operating cash flow was the one exception, worsening from negative $31.9 million to negative $34.9 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an improvement from negative $17.2 million to negative $11.3 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA also improved, going from negative $11 million to positive $20.7 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. For that time, revenue for the company actually improved. This was thanks to a strong showing in the first quarter of the year as the company benefited from a rise in ARPU. Bottom line results have gotten better, but some of that is due to a $219.7 million gain on the settlement of litigation that the company booked in the first quarter of the year. This was in relation to the $220 million payment that the company received from Disney in relation to the merger transaction. Because of this, EBITDA is the most appropriate metric here. And that does indeed show strength for the company.

It is important to keep in mind that the firm continues to make progress on improving its margins. In the image above, you can see global expenses as a percentage of total revenue for the business. They have been consistently on the decline since the third quarter of 2024 when they hit 115%. By the first quarter of this year, they had fallen to 106%. And in the most recent quarter, they amounted to 102%. This improvement seems to be centered around subscriber related costs, which management is finding ways to improve. I would venture to say that a lot of this has to do with price increases and new monetized features as opposed to actual cost cutting. Even so, progress is progress all the same.

Unlike in previous quarters, management is not really providing any detailed guidance now. But that's likely because of the upcoming merger with Disney’s Hulu + Live TV business. Management currently expects that transaction to close either in the final quarter of this year or in the first quarter of next year. At the same time, management continues to make investments in the core business. In fact, the firm will soon be launching Fubo Sports, which they have described as a "skinny content service for sports fans." They also recently launched a pay-per-view feature that allows subscribers and non-subscribers to access live events on a one-off basis. I find that to be innovative and exciting, even though I personally am not a sports fan.

The one challenge when it comes to a company like fuboTV is that we don't really know what to expect of the future. Management has not provided detailed guidance recently regarding what the post-merger business should look like. Taking from my previous article, you can see in the image above what management's prior expectations were for the combined business. Assuming that this still holds, we can expect a firm that is drastically larger than it is at the moment. Even if the $120 million in annual run rate synergies don't come to fruition, we are still looking at a business that is generating significant and growing cash flows as revenue expands.

To understand what the picture of the business might look like on a combined basis, I created the chart above. In it, you can see, both with synergies and without synergies, what the implied EV to EBITDA multiple of the company should be based on current pricing. Even if we assume that synergies don't come to fruition, the stock is not exactly expensive. And their synergies are realized, which could happen but are not guaranteed, shares could look rather cheap, especially as we move forward a couple of years. It is the realization of this value here, I believe, that has market participants so excited recently. After all, the company on a standalone basis is not that appealing. Remember that, from when I first wrote about the firm in February of 2024 until I subsequently upgraded it in January of this year, I had it rated a Sell.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I think that the market has finally come to realize the opportunity that exists with fuboTV Inc. Since I upgraded the stock, shares have risen materially. I would argue that the easy money has already been made now. But I do think that, as the merger nears completion, the firm will deserve some additional upside. Based on the data provided, I do believe that maintaining it as a soft Buy candidate is appropriate right now

data.title: Novo Nordisk: This Won't Last
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Novo Nordisk (NVO) is still an undisputed leader of the GLP-1 revolution and an all-around top pharmaceutical company.

But despite its blockbuster drugs and dominant market position, the stock has underperformed in recent months, falling nearly 15% from its all-time highs.

Some investors see this as the end of the GLP-1 trade and perhaps the beginning of some very difficult years for healthcare companies as headwinds mount.

However, I see it as a rare opportunity. With a robust pipeline, strong manufacturing expansion, and international momentum, Novo remains one of the best long-term growth stories in pharma.

Buying this stock right now is very attractive, with a +3% yield and plenty of room for capital appreciation.

This price won't last long, in my opinion.

What Happened to NVO?

NVO has been caught in a perfect storm of high expectations, sector rotation, and rising competition.

After a multi-year run that made it Europe’s most valuable company, the stock has now seen almost a 60% decline in the last year.

But the Q2 earnings may have finally shifted sentiment and given us a bottom, with the stock now up over 10% in the last two days.

Q2 Update

In Q2 2025, Novo reported revenues of DKK 65.3 billion, which is a 22% growth YoY in constant currency.

Operating profit came in at DKK 31.5 billion, up 23%. GLP-1 drugs continue to be the engine, with Wegovy sales jumping 67%, while Ozempic rose 35%.

North America remains the biggest driver, with 27% sales growth in the region, but international markets are quickly catching up. Japan, for instance, saw a 91% increase in GLP-1 sales thanks to strong Ozempic demand.

Obesity care sales nearly doubled YoY and now account for over 18% of total revenue, up from 12% last year. However, production constraints in certain regions prevented even higher growth.

Despite strong results, it’s also true that NVO trimmed full-year guidance: sales growth is now expected at 21-25%, down slightly from 22-26%.

Novo also cited higher-than-expected rebates and changing payer dynamics in Europe as potential headwinds.

And although in many ways NVO is doing great, it is true that GLP1 growth has been slowing down.

But this doesn’t change the bigger picture: demand is still far outpacing supply, and Novo is scaling rapidly to meet it.

Why I’m Doubling Down on NVO

In my humble opinion, this correction in the stock is creating a very attractive long-term entry point. Here’s why I’m buying more:

Dominance in GLP-1s

With over 65% market share in GLP-1 therapies, strong brand recognition, and years of safety data, it has a wide moat against competition.

Yes, there is stiff competition from Eli Lily (LLY) and potentially compounders like Hims (HIMS).

Moreover, Novo has shown superior performance in their latest pill version of Wegovy versus Lily, and this is very encouraging.

Wegovy is expected to launch in tablet form in 2026.

Manufacturing Expansion

Novo is investing billions to scale production. Its fill-finish capacity is expected to double by 2026, and the Clayton, NC, facility will begin delivering doses next year. This will be key to meeting pent-up demand and expanding access globally.

Strong Pipeline

Novo is developing next-gen obesity therapies like CagriSema and amylin analogues, which could offer even greater weight loss and metabolic improvements. There’s also plenty of potential for Amycretin.

GLP-1s are now being explored for a range of conditions, from Alzheimer’s to NASH to cardiovascular disease, and Novo could be at the forefront of multiple therapeutic revolutions.

The obesity treatment market is projected to grow to $100B+ annually by 2030. Novo already dominates this space, and there’s also room to grow in the rare diseases segment.

Valuation

NVO trades at around 12x forward earnings and 4x forward sales, and the company is deploying capital efficiently.

The stock is now undervalued compared to its history on a lot of metrics, and I believe it won’t take long to recover.

Risks

With that said, we can’t deny there’s a reason the stock has fallen so much in the last year, and risks remain.

On the valuation front, it is true that the forward Price/Cash flow is very rich. Indeed, NVO is going through a process of heavy investment while it struggles with margin and pricing pressures.

In this regard, President Trump has clearly waged war on pharmaceuticals, and Novo could still take a hit over the next year or two.

There’s also a risk that supply won’t keep up with demand, and competitors may seize the opportunity.

Final Thoughts

Novo is still firing on all cylinders, yet trading at a discount relative to its growth and long-term potential.

GLP-1s are changing medicine, and Novo is ahead of the competition.

With new facilities coming online, oral drugs in development, and global demand still exploding, I see NVO not only rebounding but potentially doubling in the next few years.

I’m buying more.

data.title: Dorchester Minerals: High-Yielding Oil Royalty Compounder
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Dorchester Minerals (NASDAQ:DMLP) is a mid-size oil and gas royalty company, acquiring, owning, and managing oil and natural gas royalty properties and leasing them to operators such as ExxonMobil (XOM), Diamondback (FANG), ConocoPhillips (COP), Occidental Petroleum (OXY), and many more, with a majority of their reserves in the Permian and Bakken zones.

They don't do the actual drilling or extraction themselves, but instead own the rights to the minerals in the ground and get a share of the revenue when other companies produce oil and gas from those properties.

In 2024, they recorded a very significant 33% YoY change in their oil reserves and 7% in natural gas, thanks to significant acquisitions and revisions, replacing 179% of 2024’s production. This will have an effect on future revenues, and it’s a key aspect for this company’s development.

As such, we can see quite impressive compounding in their revenues and operating income. From the lows back in 2015, we are looking at a 5x in revenue back in 2024 and a roughly 7x increase in operating income, proving this company to be a very solid compounder over the oil cycle. Still, don’t forget that they will still ultimately depend on the price of oil, as I will mention below.

Similarly, the free cash flow has been very solid since 2022, as a result of better oil prices and acquisitions. Currently, the TTM free cash flow places them at a P/FCF ratio of ~12, as a result of very solid numbers and strong oil prices. But the key here is looking at the per-share data. If we look at their free cash flow per share, which decreased about 20% from 2022 to 2024, and we can see some jumps followed by stagnation/declines before jumping again, as the company’s own investment cycles tend to depend on oil prices. That’s because their acquisitions aren’t instantly accretive to the cash flow (they won’t release what they buy immediately into production), but instead should support higher levels for the long term. Also, what this translates to on top of this is the fact that their cash flows would’ve naturally dropped on a comparable basis, which is expected given the price of oil and might continue in the future.

Still, we are talking about a cyclical company that’s currently trading at near record-level Price/FCF ratios. If they drop to the average levels of even less than 6 we’ve seen during the bad times on the back of oil dropping to similar levels, that by itself is a ~50% crash in the stock price, getting to similar levels to what they were at during the previous downcycle (mid-to-high teens).

As we can see from their Q2 report, the net income per common unit dropped nearly 60% in Q2’25 compared to Q2’24, as a result of a much lower net income combined with a higher number of common units, as I’ll mention below.

That decline in the per-share FCF comes because, in recent years, the company has been able to buy very significant amounts of properties in return for stock. 2024 actually came with their largest deals, for which the company had to issue ~18% of its shares outstanding at the time. This ensures their long-term growth, and I believe it should be seen differently from normal dilution. Since the company is a limited partnership, they aim for having zero debt (as mentioned below) to limit the liability on their general partner (this is not a limited liability company from their perspective, only for us). Still, considering the timing of this issuance, when the stock is trading at relatively high prices compared to its past, I believe this management made the right decision. In a time when others do buybacks at all-time highs, using the stock trading at upcycle levels to develop is not a bad decision, and I would say it’s actually a plus for the management.

Financially, based on their earnings report, we see an exceptional position, with no debt and the current assets covering their total liabilities more than 10 times! However, as I mentioned above, this is not really a cheap point for them. Looking at the partnership capital (similar to the equity in a normal company), we can see $319.8 million, which is traded at a market cap of $1.26 billion today. Sure, the balance sheet is backward-looking, and it does not reflect the current value of their assets, but we are most likely still looking at a much higher price to net asset value, especially now with oil prices in the ~$65/bbl area, when oil companies begin to face issues.

Shareholder Returns

Similar to the stock, the dividend has been very volatile, depending on the price of oil, as it depends on the cash receipts from its royalty interests. We see higher dividends as a result of the post-2020 jump, which is steadily declining now, as the company even announced recently when they indicated a $0.6202 dividend for Q2, bringing the forward yield to ~9% today. However, due to reasons I’ll mention in detail below, we might see even lower dividends from Q3 onward, and Q4+ will also depend significantly on oil’s performance. This kind of cut could result in the drop in stock price I mentioned above, getting the company to a more “appropriate” near-downcycle level.

Since they are a limited partnership, their cash distributions are treated differently for tax purposes. For more information about their dividends, K-1 tax reporting, and other distribution-related questions, the company has an FAQ page, which I recommend you check out here, as there is too much specific information to cover in the analysis.

A key thing to remember about this business model is that they don’t have to worry about being constrained by the need to save cash for future drilling/exploration projects or upgrades, as their purpose is to pass on the cash they receive from royalties to their investors. Last year, we were looking at a ~150% payout ratio based on the free cash flow, which is not sustainable, but they still have some cash on the balance sheet they can use to sustain a higher dividend, but that ~$36.51 million in cash likely won’t last for too long in my view, and we already see the recent dividend lowered.

Risks & Opportunities

One key thing to remember when investing in oil companies is that $65/bbl already gets into troubling times for many oil producers, with $60 generally being considered the “floor” from which many of them start cutting CAPEX, dividends/buybacks, etc., and go into a self-preservation mode. But, as a result of the upcoming cuts in CAPEX and investments, we’re likely to see oil prices rebound over time (especially after OPEC+ stops the production hikes), which can be great for DMLP and other oil-related stocks, as they generally follow the price of oil with leverage. Still, I believe we might see some short-term pressure if tariff uncertainty continues (Trump has very recently threatened 35% tariffs on the EU after the recent deal if they breach it). However, I believe we should see better oil prices in the long term for several reasons, other than OPEC+ and tariff uncertainty fading away, but keep in mind that oil prices are still a guessing game in the end, as nobody can predict them.

The advantage that DMLP has is its significant exposure to the Permian and Bakken, which are arguably the best areas for oil and gas in the US. As new wells are expected to be more and more expensive in terms of production, this can lift oil prices up in the long term, especially as “cheap oil” is running out, which pushed oil giants such as Exxon and Chevron (CVX) into countries like Guyana, where they plan very significant investments. The value of their claims could be even more significant in the future if these reserves are running out, so there is also potential to see some appreciation in that regard.

For example, a recent Dallas Fed Energy Survey showed that to profitably drill a new well in the Permian Basin, companies needed prices of ~$64 per barrel, which is very close to the level we are at today:

For the entire sample, firms need $64 per barrel on average to profitably drill, higher than the $62-per-barrel price when this question was asked last year. Across regions, average breakeven prices to profitably drill range from $59 to $70 per barrel. Breakeven prices in the Permian Basin average $65 per barrel, $4 higher than last year. Almost all firms in the survey can profitably drill a new well at current prices. (The WTI spot price was $83 per barrel during the survey period.)

Today, things may look very different, and we might see several producers already struggling when it comes to the profitability of their new wells, which can result in even more limited investments in production expansion and exploration, which can affect entire industries.

What this can translate into is higher average prices in the future, as the US has a significant influence on the price both as a consumer and producer. Even if OPEC+ will play around with the production in the short term (perhaps to counter Trump’s tariffs and other pro-oil drilling plans), they are also losing on potential profits by doing this because they can always just continue to sell at the international prices they apply (Saudi Aramco, for example, applies different rates compared to the base depending on where they sell, which is normal).

This gets to another important aspect regarding the supply and demand balance: the very high amount of mergers and acquisitions we’ve seen at all levels in recent years. This consolidation adds nothing to the total supply, and at best, it generates some synergies and better margins for the oil companies, but the effect on the supply of oil remains very limited. To get an idea, virtually every oil giant has been undergoing at least one significant merger/acquisition in the past years: Exxon's (XOM) Pioneer deal, Chevron buying Hess, Occidental + CrownRock, ConocoPhillips + Marathon Oil, etc.

Also, a key point to remember about oil wells in particular is the combination of paraffin wax, asphaltenes, and mineral scales that make it hard and unsafe to close a well. These substances accumulate and create blockages that restrict the flow and prevent valves from closing properly, leading to significant operational challenges and the potential to damage the well itself. Instead, oil producers are often going for storing and waiting for better prices, an industry that exploded when oil futures fell into the negative back in 2020, thanks to record levels of demand for storing services in a very short time.

They also talk about a relatively unique aspect of this kind of industry: the lag between production and cash receipts. Why that is an issue is because this delay can spread over multiple months as a result of adjustments, suspense releases, etc., and the prices can change dramatically during this time. In the example above, they show how a typical cash receipt cycle looks like, assuming production in January, where we can see how the cash begins to flow over the next couple of months and can extend all the way to August, meaning a 7-month cycle for receiving the full payments, with most of it coming in the first 2 months. What this means is we can see a small delay in good or bad times compared to oil producers—enough to have an effect on the next quarter, depending on the timing.

So, just because they report a good quarter when oil companies have issues, it doesn’t mean they won’t follow; it’s just a delayed reaction. For reference, in their most recent (second quarter) dividend distribution, they say how 66% of Q2’s distribution’s receipts reflect oil sales and natural gas sales during February 2025 through April 2025, and 34% come from prior sales periods, which means they barely felt the effects of lower oil prices we’ve seen since April! This is why this delay is important to keep in mind, and we might see the issues begin from Q3 onward, depending on the price of oil.

In a way, the fact that this is a limited partnership also comes with an emphasis on it being quite a passive type of investment—assuming you don’t mind the volatility—as the limited partners (unitholders, meaning people who buy this stock) don’t have a say in the day-to-day operations of the company (that goes to the general partner, Dorchester Minerals Management LP—common practice for limited partnerships), but are entitled to its cash distributions.

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers such as Sitio Royalties (STR), Black Stone Minerals (BSM), Kimbell Royalty (KRP), Northern Oil and Gas (NOG), Viper Energy (VNOM), and Freehold Royalties (OTCPK:FRHLF), we see once again the issues that I mentioned above. Their PB is the highest in this group, and so is the P/CF ratio, as a result of the elevated valuation. The PB may not reflect the net asset value or quality, but the P/CF is hard to ignore. Even the other ratios are at best similar to their peers, which might be an indicator that DMLP is at least traded at more favorable valuations than their peers, which may not be sustainable.

Valuation

At the end of the day, the stock’s performance and dividend will be tied to the price of oil. To adjust for their acquisitions, number of shares, and ~14.5% lower oil prices compared to 2024’s average, I believe we can expect DMLP to make somewhere around $80 million this year if oil prices remain at the current levels, as they don’t have debt and don’t have to worry about CAPEX. Again, remember that oil prices will dictate this at the end of the day, so volatility is a given.

To adjust for the volatility, let’s assume a CAGR of only 6% over the next 5 years, followed by 4% for the subsequent 5, as the company has proven to be very willing to take advantage of a higher stock price to develop, which will usually happen during the upcycle (the past 10-year CAGR in FCF/share should be around ~10.85% based on the $80 million expected), but accounting for an overall weaker environment due to the smaller profits of new wells.

Assuming a terminal growth rate of 2% and a WACC of 8%—in line with market estimates—the resulting DCF model gets us to an enterprise value of ~$1.68 billion. After adding the $36.51 million in net cash, this leads to an equity value of ~$1.717 billion. With 47.34 million shares outstanding, this gives an intrinsic value of about $36.26 per share, leaving a decent margin of safety from the current level. However, this assumes growing cash flows going forward, which is very unlikely due to oil’s volatility, as this is definitely not the bottom.

Conclusions

Overall, given their exceptional financial health and relatively low valuation, that still leaves a margin of safety and a good dividend. I rate DMLP a Buy now. However, keep in mind that volatility is very likely, and there is definitely potential to see prices fall even more, as the company is still traded higher than its peers. But it should remain a solid long-term investment and a good way to get leverage on the price of oil, especially if averaging down at better prices when possible.

data.title: Palantir Cannot Be Caught
data.description:
Palantir Technologies Inc.'s (NASDAQ:PLTR) vertically integrated technology stack is turning data gravity into customer gravity. The company's single Apollo control plain, Ontology layer, and five-day AIP Boot Camps create an increasingly powerful flywheel in which every new workflow increases lock-in for existing customers and reduces friction for future customers. As GenAI explodes, this flywheel is only going to spin faster: Palantir's AIP already powers $1 billion in commercial run-rate, while its 10-year Army framework is worth up to $10 billion. Palantir's growing influence in the commercial sector and increasingly secure position as the nation's mission-critical AI backbone puts the company in an incredibly advantageous position moving forward.

The Architecture That Seeds Network Effects

Ontology: A Common Language for Data and Agents

The core, and perhaps most integral part of the Palantir flywheel, is Ontology. Ontology allows for a live digital twin that carefully maps a customer's workflows, assets, and even people into a common semantic model. What's makes this even more powerful is its ability to be permission-shared across organizations. This means that each complementation contributes entirely new entities, heuristics, and relationships that future customers can inherent and benefit from. Over time, this expanding library of ontologies becomes an unsurmountable moat, as switching vendors would mean recreating years of ecosystem-building and semantic context.

Apollo: Write Once, Deploy Anywhere

Next comes Apollo, which is a single control plane that has the ability to push the same application build to a large network of hosting sites, from hyperscalers like Amazon (AMZN) AWS and Microsoft (MSFT) Azure, to on-prem clusters, disconnected submarines, and even air-gapped intelligence networks. Updates and security patches therefore benefit all environments simultaneously, which only further compounds the value of the installed base.

A Self-Reinforcing Commercial Flywheel

AIP Boot Camps

Palantir's five-day AIP Boot Camp serves as the foundation for the company's go-to-market strategy. Prospective customers enter the boot camp with raw data and often leaves with production-grade workflows. The success of early adopters sends strong signals to future customers that Palantir's platform is enterprise-ready. By showcasing success stories from past AIP Boot Camp with large enterprises like Heineken (HEINY), Walgreens (WBA), and AT&T (T), Palantir only further increases the demand for its commercial business.

Abnormally High Net-Dollar-Retention

Perhaps the single most important metric for Palantir's increasingly strong customer gravity is its reported 128% net-dollar retention. This figure is essentially proof that existing customers are buying more seats, workloads, and AIP agents every year. The company's customer count itself grew 64% YoY to 849, with U.S. commercial revenue growing an astounding 93%. Each expansion feeds into Palantir's increasingly vast Ontology library and helps fund more product R&D, further tightening the loop.

Government Backbone Strengthens the Loop

Palantir's public-sector franchise still acts as the company's backbone and central nervous system. As an example of how much sway Palantir has garnered in Government, the U.S. Army awarded Palantir with a contract worth $10 billion over ten years. Because the same Ontology and Apollo layers also serve classified workloads, the security accreditation earned in the public sector further serves to harden Palantir's products for highly sensitive commercial industries like pharma companies, insurance, banks, and utilities. The additional an of increasingly entrenched public business allows for a two-sided spillover network effect that rivals without deep government ties will likely find increasingly hard to match.

GenAI's Blistering Pace Plays to Palantir's Strengths

A Secular Market That Doubles Every Three Years

GenAI usage is starting to explode across nearly all major domains. Both OpenAI and Anthropic have seen their usage rate approximately quadruple over the past few quarters. Even firms like IDC and McKinsey are estimating are estimating that GenAI will continue to experience explosive growth, with the latter estimating that GenAI could unlock $2.6-4.4 trillion in annual economic value. However, this speed of adoption is expected to race far ahead of governance, with directors ranking data security and model control as primary GenAI concerns.

Why Governance Concerns Favor Palantir

The growing GenAI governance concerns play directly into Palantir's skillset. The company's AIP product offers fine-grained access controls, lineage tracking, and a host of other security features that allow enterprises to embrace GenAI without violating their compliance regimes. As models continue to iterate from Claude's Sonnet-class LLMs to multimodal agents capable of taking over entire workflows, Palantir is able to roll out new model capabilities to multiple environments in a single click, all while preserving lineage and permissions. Therefore, each incremental algorithm increases Palantir's network effects.

Financial Compounding: Network Effects in the P&L

Despite the increasingly absurd expectations surrounding Palantir, the company is somehow still managing to blow out expectations. In Q2, Palantir grew its revenue 49% YoY to $1.004 billion, which beat consensus and prompted management to lift full-year guidance to $4.15 billion. Notably, the company's free cash flow came in at $569 million. This gives Palantir's Rule-of-40 score a 94.

Palantir now has $5.4 billion of cash with zero debt, which means that it can aggressively fund R&D to further push its lead. Every dollar of incremental software revenue drops through at high incremental margins. This translates to an Ontology-Apollo-API network that becomes even more profitable as it scales up.

Competitive Moat and Optionality

The success of Palantir will inevitably draw in far greater competition. Hyperscalers like Microsoft and Amazon are already attempting to move into this space. However, these competitors are still largely offering generic model APIs, unlike the end-to-end operating system that Palantir is able to provide. Moreover, Palantir has cleared facilities and twenty-year battlefield credibility to its name, which acts as a large differentiator. Moving forward, Palantir will be able to monetize on its network in new ways: revenue-sharing marketplaces for third-party agents, usage-based Apollo licensing for non-Palantir workloads, and verticalized "AIP-as-a-Service" bundles. Each adjacent use-case leverages the same core flywheel.

Risks

While Palantir is well on its way to become one of the most transformative companies in the world, its high valuation still presents obvious risks. As such, even a slight slowdown in growth could cause the company's valuation to crash. The company currently trades at a mind-boggling P/E ratio of 577, which is even more stunning for a company already valued at $409 billion. Still, the drivers that could dent demand, like tighter budgets and stricter regulations, also raise the bar for security, auditability and ROI. These are all areas where Palantir outperforms.

Government revenue concentration also presents a large risk. If the U.S. Government decides to pull back on even a few contracts, this could huge a large negative impact on revenue. However, the new ten-year Army contract brings a degree of visibility that Palantir has never had before. Moreover, the company's commercial mix has grown to 44% and will likely continue to grow moving forward.

Conclusion

Network effects are often an overused term in tech, but in Palantir's case, it undersells the magnitude of its flywheels. The company has a growing Ontology that lower time-to-value, an Apollo control plane that can deploy innovation immediately, and a defense franchise that embeds the company inside some of the U.S. most important institutions. The generative-AI gold rush only accelerates these advances since much of Palantir's value-add is dependent upon highly performance underlying models. Palantir is poised to become the operating system of the AI century and is building a wall that competitors will likely find increasingly hard to scale. Even at a valuation of $423 billion, Palantir still has far more room to run.

data.title: Crane NXT, Co. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Crane NXT, Co. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Atlassian: Another Dip, Another Chance To Buy
data.description:
We're now in the thick of the Q2 earnings season, and several trends are becoming clear so far in the second half of a volatile 2025. To me, one of the principal shifts is the fact that investors seem to be losing confidence in small- and mid-cap stocks, throwing in the towel and bidding up large-cap winners like Microsoft (MSFT), Palantir (PLTR), and Meta (META).

Investors have less confidence, meanwhile, that AI will also be a benefit to smaller software companies like Atlassian (NASDAQ:TEAM), the collaboration and workflow tool that has lost ~30% of its value since the start of the year - despite Atlassian's agentic AI releases and its strong growth at scale. The company just reported fiscal Q4 (June quarter) results, and investors turned up their nose at Atlassian's guidance for the upcoming FY26, sending shares down further. The question now: Is Atlassian headed for a further correction, or is this dip a buying opportunity?

I think it's the latter. I last wrote a buy opinion on Atlassian in May, when the stock was hovering above $200 per share. Now, it's important to note that I'm not a price-agnostic investor: I was very skeptical on the stock when it was hovering in the high $200s/above $300 earlier this year. But now, with Atlassian having shed ~half of its value in a short amount of time, I believe the market has overreacted to the decelerating-growth narrative that has overtaken this stock. In my view, the stock has become incredibly undervalued, especially for its new suite of AI tools as well as its profit expansion. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here and recommending buying the dip while Atlassian is trading at rare low multiples.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to Atlassian, here are the core reasons to be bullish on the stock:

  • Especially as AI expands its use cases, Atlassian is still in the earlier stages of capturing a broad market opportunity. The company estimates its TAM at $67 billion, consisting of a $35 billion market in work management, $17 billion in software development, and $15 billion in IT service management. Its current revenue run rate is still only less than ~10% penetrated into this industry in which Atlassian remains a market leader.
  • Unique sales model leading to rich margins. Atlassian is a true outlier in the enterprise software sector by focusing more on inbound rather than outbound sales. It doesn't have an army of account executives "pushing" its products; rather, it relies on word of mouth and customer expansion. This has allowed Atlassian to build up rich profit margins earlier in its lifespan, which it has sustained at scale.
  • Sky high gross margins. Atlassian's mid-80%s pro forma gross margins are among the highest in the industry, which also allows for significant economies of scale especially with the slimmer spending on sales.
  • Successful subscription transition. Last year, Atlassian killed off support for its final license products, having now successfully transitioned all of its customers into recurring cloud subscriptions, stabilizing its revenue base.
  • Rule of 40. Atlassian combines ~20% growth with a mid-20%s operating margin, putting the stock firmly in the "Rule of 40" category that helps justify a thinning valuation premium.

Speaking of valuation: Despite Atlassian's fundamental strengths, the stock's ~30% slide since the start of the year has nearly tipped the stock into value territory. At current post-earnings share prices near $171, Atlassian trades at a $44.89 billion market cap. After we net off the $2.93 billion of cash and $987.7 million of debt on Atlassian's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $42.95 billion.

For FY26 (which is the fiscal year that Atlassian just kicked off, ending in June 2026), the company is guiding to 18% y/y revenue growth, which implies $6.15 billion in revenue.

We note that part of the reason Atlassian shares tumbled post-earnings is because Wall Street had been hoping for 19% growth for FY26. That being said, we should recognize that Atlassian's guidance philosophy has a bent toward conservatism. For FY25, Atlassian had originally guided to 16% revenue growth but instead, delivered 20% growth. Atlassian is leaving room for "beat and raise" quarters ahead. It's premature to judge the company for its disappointing guidance now.

Still, if we take the current guidance at face value, Atlassian trades at 7.0x EV/FY26 revenue. We note two things: first, despite the fact that Atlassian's pace of growth has barely decelerated (from the ~20%s) over the past few years, the stock has rarely sat at a single-digit revenue multiple. Second, we can stack up Atlassian against a number of similar Rule of 40 software stocks that are growing in the ~20% range like ServiceNow (NOW), JFrog (FROG), HubSpot (HUBS) - and we find that the stock now sits at a substantial discount, especially to the likes of ServiceNow (which actually operates in a very adjacent IT service management space).

Take advantage of this (in my view, temporary) dislocation between fundamental performance and value in Atlassian as a well-timed buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Atlassian's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Atlassian's revenue grew 22% y/y to $1.38 billion, which smashed Wall Street's more conservative expectations of $1.35 billion (+20% y/y) by a two-point margin. Also of note, as shown in the chart below, is the fact that Atlassian's revenue accelerated 8 points y/y versus 14% y/y growth in Q3.

Now, we do note that a large chunk of the acceleration is due to the fact that Q3 had a tougher comp from the sunsetting of Atlassian's server products last year, which spurred a wave of last-time buys in Q3'24. License/server products are now fully out of Atlassian's prior-year compares, putting the focus on the company's cloud revenue streams. Still, we note that while it's not eight points, cloud revenue also accelerated one point to 26% y/y growth in Q4. We note as well that the company's marketplace segment (which features apps by third-party developers intended to integrate into Atlassian systems) rebounded to mid-teens growth.

In particular, we note that AI is helping to drive major wins for Atlassian that we aren't necessarily seeing yet in the company's financials. The company notes that there are now 2.3 million active Rovo AI agents deployed on the Atlassian platform, which increased ~50% sequentially over 1.5 million at the end of Q3. The company also notes that it signed a record number of deals with over $1 million in annual contract value, and that this large-deal count is up 2x y/y over the prior-year Q4.

One of the principal concerns that investors have against Atlassian is the possibility that AI begins to take over more software development jobs. As a work collaboration tool that is aimed primarily at technical users, reduced headcount of software developers would directly impact Atlassian's seat counts and slow down its growth.

When pressed on this possibility on the Q4 earnings call, however, CEO and founder Mike Cannon-Brookes notes that so far, code generation tools have not at all slowed down Atlassian's business or threatened its key customer base:

Let me just start by saying we're not seeing any impact from this on any numbers that we have. So expansion rates, growth rates, adoption rates of our technical products. And again, the technology teams and the software business is a part of our business alongside service strategy and broad collaboration. But we're not seeing any impact from it. So we're being very clear about that. When customers integrate code-generating AI tools, there's no change in their usage of our products, and we continue to see really healthy user growth.

In fact, we even have some early data that shows that when people do connect cogeneration tools, the business users in those geo instances is actually growing faster than they are for the customers that don't have those connected, right, demonstrating, we think, Jira continue to be the center gravity in how work gets done [...]

Software creation is becoming more efficient. Cost of software building going down. This is all really good for Atlassian and our workflow tools. Again, in that software teams and technology teams world that we sit in."

We should also point out that these same trends (fewer software developers and technical users), if true, should also impact other IT management or DevOps tools, such as ServiceNow and JFrog - stocks that are trading at significantly richer multiples than Atlassian.

On the profitability front, Atlassian delivered significant margin expansion as well. As shown in the chart below, pro forma operating margins jumped 470bps yesterday/y to 24.3%:

We note this firmly places Atlassian in the "Rule of 40" category, balancing mid-20%s growth alongside mid-20%s margins. The company's guidance, calling for 24% full-year margins in FY26 alongside 18% revenue growth (which I view to be conservative on both the top and bottom line) also sustains that status.

Key takeaways

To be clear, Atlassian is an investment that has required patience this year, and it will likely be several quarters until the company can regain investors' trust. In my view, the company is well-positioned to execute "beat and raise" quarters in FY26 as its guidance calls for significant deceleration (which plays into investors' fears that AI is a net headwind, not a tailwind to Atlassian). That being said, Atlassian continues to print healthy metrics, including 120% net revenue retention rates that indicate very healthy upsells and spending activity on its platform.

Stay long here and take advantage of discounted multiples to buy.
